title: Source lineage for CME00113
summary: CME00113 is the repository XML representation of the title matter and printed pp. 1–93 of an AMS Press 1968 reprint of John Robson's 1842 Camden Society edition of three independent Middle English romances. Robson's principal manuscript was Princeton Taylor MS. 9. He mediated selected Anturs readings through Frederick Madden's 1839 edition and its Lincoln/Douce collation, and supplied Sir Amadace stanza XXXII from Henry Weber's 1810 edition of the NLS witness. Complete provider-published image inventories are cached for Taylor MS. 9 and NLS Adv.MS.19.3.1; the Bodleian public object has only two Douce 324 images, and no complete public Lincoln MS 91 or Douce 324 facsimile was found.
review_notes: The three medieval works, four manuscript witnesses, three nineteenth-century intermediary editions, the AMS reprint, institutional facsimiles, digital encoding, and repository artifact are distinct layers.
Complete image coverage means the complete provider-published canvas inventory, not every physical blank leaf, edge, spine, or preservation-master exposure.

Standalone Princeton and NLS Canvas IDs returned HTTP 404 on 2026-07-12; exact verified Image API URLs are therefore used for human-facing work-portion boundaries, while canvas IDs remain inventory locators.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00113
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by the repository conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID are all CME00113.
The XML retains the original 1842 title page nested inside the AMS Press reprint title matter.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English item CME00113
entities.description: Electronic text encoding the AMS reprint title matter and Robson's three poem texts on printed pp. 1–93. The introduction, manuscript description, post-text notes, glossary, corrigenda, and other preliminaries are omitted.

entities.label: AMS Press 1968 reprint of Robson's Three Early English Metrical Romances
entities.description: The immediate printed material source identified by the U-M encoding's reprint title page. It reproduces Robson's 1842 Camden Society edition and adds AMS Press title matter.

bibliographic.citation: John Robson, ed., Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary (New York and London: AMS Press, 1968), reprint of the 1842 Camden Society edition.
bibliographic.title: Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary

notes: The reprint title page states that it was issued with permission of the Royal Historical Society.

entities.label: Robson's 1842 Three Early English Metrical Romances
entities.description: John Robson's Camden Society edition of The Anturs of Arther, Sir Amadace, and The Avowynge of King Arther, principally transcribed from the manuscript now Princeton Taylor MS. 9 and supplemented from printed intermediary editions.

bibliographic.citation: John Robson, ed., Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary, Camden Society Old Series 18 (London: John Bowyer Nichols and Son for the Camden Society, 1842).
bibliographic.title: Three Early English Metrical Romances: With an Introduction and Glossary
bibliographic.publisher: John Bowyer Nichols and Son for the Camden Society

physical_description.extent: Introduction and manuscript description followed by 132 numbered pages
notes: Poems occupy pp. 1–93; notes, glossary, and corrigenda follow.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Robson's 1842 edition
entities.description: Complete public scan contributed by the University of California Libraries, with derived OCR and item metadata.
entities.reproduction_format: PDF page images with derived OCR and metadata

entities.label: Princeton University Library, Taylor MS. 9
entities.description: Composite fifteenth-century manuscript containing the principal Robson witnesses: Awntyrs ff. 1r–15v, the surviving acephalous Sir Amadace ff. 17r–34v, and Avowynge ff. 34v–59r.

date_statements.label: Manuscript production
date_statements.notes: Current Princeton catalog dating; this is not a composition date for the romances.

holding.notes: Robert H. Taylor Collection of English and American Literature.

notes: Written in Anglicana; the manuscript combines romances with later Manor of Hale records.
A modern paper note at f. 16r records loss of the original leaf and the beginning of Sir Amadace.

entities.label: Princeton digital facsimile of Taylor MS. 9
entities.description: Complete provider-published 207-canvas microfilm-derived IIIF sequence, from front cover through back cover.
entities.reproduction_format: IIIF Presentation 2 sequence of microfilm-derived JPEG images

entities.label: Madden's 1839 Syr Gawayne edition
entities.description: Frederic Madden's printed edition of Arthurian poems. Its Awntyrs text on pp. 95–128 uses Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 as base and records Douce 324 variants; Robson used this printed intermediary for selected Anturs readings.

bibliographic.citation: Frederic Madden, ed., Syr Gawayne: A Collection of Ancient Romance-Poems by Scotish and English Authors, Relating to that Celebrated Knight of the Round Table (London: Richard and John E. Taylor for the Bannatyne Club, 1839).
bibliographic.title: Syr Gawayne
bibliographic.publisher: Richard and John E. Taylor for the Bannatyne Club

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Madden's 1839 Syr Gawayne
entities.description: Complete public Cornell-contributed scan and item metadata.

entities.label: Lincoln Cathedral Library, MS 91
entities.description: Robert Thornton's paper manuscript, produced in North Yorkshire c. 1430–1450. Awntyrs occupies ff. 154r–161r and is Madden's base witness.

notes: Written by Robert Thornton; one leaf is missing between current ff. 157 and 158, and f. 159 is damaged.

entities.label: University of Lincoln selected image of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91, f. 80r
entities.description: One publicly delivered partial-folio image linked from the official catalog record. It shows Morte Arthure at f. 80r and has zero overlap with Awntyrs at ff. 154r–161r.
entities.reproduction_format: One JPEG crop of manuscript f. 80r
notes: The official record states: MS 91 f.80r part; Copyright Lincoln Cathedral Library; permission of the Dean and Chapter of Lincoln Cathedral.

entities.label: Bodleian Library, MS. Douce 324
entities.description: Fifteenth-century English paper manuscript containing Awntyrs. The current catalog infers the work at ff. 1r–14v; DIMEV gives ff. 1r–11v, an unresolved locator discrepancy.

entities.label: Digital Bodleian two-image object for MS. Douce 324
entities.description: The complete currently published IIIF object consists of only fol. 1r and fol. 4v. Both images overlap Awntyrs, but they do not form continuous or complete coverage of the manuscript or work.

entities.label: Weber's 1810 Metrical Romances, volume 3
entities.description: Volume 3 of Henry Weber's Metrical Romances, containing Sir Amadas on pp. 241–275 from NLS Advocates MS 19.3.1. Robson supplied Sir Amadace stanza XXXII from this edition.

bibliographic.citation: Henry Weber, ed., Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries, vol. 3 (Edinburgh: George Ramsay and Company for Archibald Constable and Company, 1810).
bibliographic.title: Metrical Romances of the Thirteenth, Fourteenth, and Fifteenth Centuries
bibliographic.publisher: George Ramsay and Company for Archibald Constable and Company

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of Weber's Metrical Romances, volume 3
entities.description: Complete public scan of the 1810 volume.

entities.label: National Library of Scotland, Advocates MS 19.3.1
entities.description: Paper manuscript dated circa 1480. Its acephalous Sir Amadas occupies ff. 68r–84r and was Weber's source witness.

notes: The Sir Amadas witness is acephalous.

entities.label: NLS digital facsimile of Advocates MS 19.3.1
entities.description: Complete provider-published 482-canvas IIIF sequence. The provider notes that not all modern blank flyleaves were captured; Sir Amadas ff. 68r–84r maps to canvases 151–183.

entities.label: Digital Index of Middle English Verse release v1.0
entities.description: Versioned scholarly catalog data describing the three works and their manuscript witnesses, including DIMEV records 2628, 5552, and 1885.

subject.description: Acephalous Sir Amadace except supplied stanza XXXII

scope.notes: The leaf before surviving f. 17r is lost. Robson says it contained the first four twelve-line stanzas (48 lines), so his 72 renumbered surviving or supplied stanzas are not a full-work extent.

object.description: Madden's Awntyrs edition and Lincoln/Douce apparatus

scope.notes: Robson acknowledges Madden's extensive assistance and a 1839 collation. This relation does not assert Robson's direct consultation of Lincoln MS 91 or Douce 324.

subject.description: Robson's Anturs stanza XXXIX and stanza LIII line 5

object.description: Douce readings printed in Madden's apparatus

scope.notes: Exact textual comparison shows Robson reconstructed the Douce text from Madden's printed variant apparatus.

rights.component: Modern repository XML structure and markup
rights.basis: No repository-file license or item-specific rights statement was found for the modern XML markup; this does not alter the public-domain status of the underlying medieval texts and 1842 edition.
The U-M Corpus of Middle English collection applies No Copyright – United States to its public content; institutional service terms still apply.

rights.component: AMS Press reprint title matter, typography, and reproduction layer
rights.basis: The reprint was published in 1968 and states that it was issued with permission; no item-specific license or copyright renewal analysis was located. The underlying 1842 edition remains public domain in the United States.
First published in 1842, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying edition is public domain, but the item metadata supplies no item-specific license for the scan files; Internet Archive service terms apply.

rights.component: Underlying medieval texts embodied in Taylor MS. 9
rights.basis: The manuscript and its medieval texts predate modern copyright; ownership of the physical codex and reproduction terms are separate questions.
The current IIIF manifest and DPUL record state Copyright Not Evaluated; no reuse license is granted by that statement.
First published in 1839, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and derivatives
rights.basis: The underlying edition is public domain, but the item metadata supplies no item-specific scan-file license; Internet Archive service terms apply.

rights.component: Underlying medieval text; future order-specific reproductions excluded
rights.basis: The medieval text is public domain. Lincoln Cathedral's fee and any order-specific reproduction conditions govern supplied files separately and are not publication authorization.

rights.component: University of Lincoln catalog JPEG of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 f. 80r part
rights.basis: The official record explicitly states Copyright Lincoln Cathedral Library and permission of the Dean and Chapter of Lincoln Cathedral; no open reuse license is stated.

rights.component: Underlying medieval text; future order-specific reproductions excluded
rights.basis: The medieval text is public domain. Physical access and any supplied-image terms are separate and require confirmation for each order.

rights.component: Two Digital Bodleian images and their delivered derivatives
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the images to Bodleian Libraries and applies Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 4.0; Digital Bodleian terms also apply.
First published in 1810, well before the current United States public-domain cutoff for published works.
The underlying edition is public domain, but no item-specific scan-file license was established; Internet Archive service terms apply.

rights.component: Underlying medieval text embodied in Adv.MS.19.3.1
rights.basis: The manuscript and medieval text predate modern copyright; the digital reproduction layer is separately recorded.
The NLS IIIF manifest marks the digital object with the Public Domain Mark and states that it is free of known copyright restrictions. The mark is not a license.
The versioned repository licenses DIMEV data under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0.

editorial_practices.description: Robson says he expanded the manuscript's usual contractions while otherwise attempting a literal copy.
Robson reports supplying punctuation and adding hyphens and accents to aid readers; these are editorial, not manuscript features.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Gaps or defective readings in Taylor MS. 9
editorial_practices.representation: Selected supplied readings from Madden 1839 and Weber 1810
editorial_practices.description: Anturs stanza XXXIX and stanza LIII line 5 derive from Madden's printed Douce apparatus; Sir Amadace stanza XXXII derives from Weber. The intermediary editions are modeled explicitly rather than implying Robson directly consulted their manuscripts.
editorial_practices.scope: Anturs stanza XXXIX and stanza LIII line 5; Sir Amadace stanza XXXII

editorial_practices.source_feature: Complete AMS reprint of Robson's edition
editorial_practices.representation: Reprint and original title matter plus the main medieval poem texts
editorial_practices.description: The encoding includes title matter and Robson's printed pp. 1–93 but omits the introduction, manuscript description, post-text notes, glossary, corrigenda, and other preliminaries.

editorial_practices.representation: Ten apparatus quotations nested as L elements inside NOTE elements
editorial_practices.description: The raw descendant L count includes ten nested apparatus lines. Direct poem-line counts exclude those quotations and preserve incomplete stanzas rather than normalizing to ideal forms.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00113.xml.
evidence.summary: Primary repository artifact and evidence for identifiers, title matter, encoded selection, page milestones, divisions, notes, and nested apparatus lines.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CME00113.xml checked against Robson's printed edition.
evidence.locator: BODY and its three DIV2 poem divisions
evidence.summary: Counts 198 LG containers, 199 displayed stanzas, 2,726 direct poem lines, and ten additional L elements nested in Anturs apparatus notes; documents the Sir Amadace grouping anomaly and incomplete stanza forms.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English collection.
evidence.summary: Institutional collection context, public access route, and No Copyright – United States rights layer; the item stable URL returned automated HTTP 403 during review.

evidence.citation: AMS Press reprint title page encoded in CME00113 and reproduced before Robson's original 1842 title page.
evidence.summary: States reprinting with permission of the Royal Historical Society and the AMS Press New York/London imprint, proving the immediate printed material source is a reprint layer.

evidence.citation: Google Books bibliographic record for Three Early English Metrical Romances.
evidence.summary: Identifies John Robson, AMS Press, 1968, reprint status, and 132-page extent.

evidence.citation: John Robson, ed., Three Early English Metrical Romances, Camden Society Old Series 18 (1842), Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: Complete volume; poems at printed pp. 1–93 / PDF pp. 53–145
evidence.summary: Confirms bibliographic identity, poem sequence and pagination, stanza numbering, supplied-reading labels, and the edition text represented by CME00113.

evidence.citation: Robson, Three Early English Metrical Romances, introduction and Description of the Manuscript.
evidence.summary: Describes Taylor MS. 9, the lost opening four Sir Amadace stanzas, expansion and punctuation policy, Madden's extensive assistance and collation, and Weber's Advocates-manuscript edition.

evidence.summary: Identifies the University of California Libraries contribution and scan derivatives; supplies no item-specific scan-file license.

evidence.summary: Complete 207-canvas provider-published sequence, labels, exact image services, and Copyright Not Evaluated statement.

evidence.citation: Frederic Madden, ed., Syr Gawayne (1839), Internet Archive scan.
evidence.locator: Awntyrs at printed pp. 95–128 / PDF pp. 199–232
evidence.summary: Complete scan establishing Madden's Lincoln base text and Douce apparatus and providing the printed intermediary Robson used.

evidence.citation: Exact comparison of Madden's Awntyrs text and apparatus with Robson's Anturs.
evidence.summary: Shows the Douce readings from which Robson constructed Anturs stanza XXXIX and stanza LIII line 5, supporting mediation through Madden rather than unproved direct manuscript consultation.

evidence.citation: Internet Archive metadata for cu31924059937726.
evidence.summary: Identifies the Cornell-contributed scan and its derivatives; supplies no item-specific scan-file license.

evidence.citation: Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 catalog records, Lincoln Cathedral Archives and University of Lincoln Special Collections.
evidence.summary: Current reference, alternate shelfmark, 1430–1450 dating, North Yorkshire/Robert Thornton production, physical description, exact Awntyrs locus, and the f.80r representative-image identification.

evidence.citation: Lincoln Cathedral, Readers and Researchers.
evidence.summary: Explains that digital images and photocopies may be requested, with fees and repository review; no complete public MS 91 facsimile is offered there.

evidence.citation: University of Lincoln Special Collections, selected image of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 f. 80r part.
evidence.summary: Stable public 442,818-byte JPEG. The record identifies f. 80r, places it within Morte Arthure ff. 53r–98v, states Copyright Lincoln Cathedral Library and permission of the Dean and Chapter, and therefore establishes zero overlap with Awntyrs ff. 154r–161r.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Medieval Manuscripts catalog record for MS. Douce 324.
evidence.summary: Current identifiers, fifteenth-century dating, paper support, i + 14-leaf extent, and inferred Awntyrs locus ff. 1r–14v.

evidence.summary: Machine-readable official catalog evidence for identifiers, extent, dating, and content.

evidence.citation: Digital Bodleian IIIF object for MS. Douce 324.
evidence.summary: The complete public selection has only fol. 1r and fol. 4v; it is partial and noncontinuous for both manuscript and work. The manifest applies CC BY-NC 4.0.

evidence.summary: Current fee-based route for requesting complete or target-specific manuscript imaging and separate reproduction guidance.

evidence.summary: Complete scan establishing Weber's Sir Amadas edition and the passage Robson supplied as stanza XXXII.

evidence.citation: Exact comparison of Weber's Sir Amadas with Robson's Sir Amadace stanza XXXII.
evidence.summary: Confirms the twelve-line passage copied by Robson and separates that supply from the lost forty-eight-line opening of the Taylor witness.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland Archives and Manuscripts record for Adv.MS.19.3.1.

open_questions.question: Did Robson directly consult Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 or Bodleian MS. Douce 324, or did all non-Taylor Anturs readings reach him through Madden's 1839 edition and assistance?
next_steps: Search Robson and Madden correspondence, Camden Society papers, and library reader records for explicit consultation evidence.
Until direct access is proved, retain Madden 1839 as the printed intermediary and do not add Robson-to-Lincoln or Robson-to-Douce manuscript edges.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV locate Awntyrs in MS. Douce 324 at ff. 1r–11v while the current Bodleian catalog infers ff. 1r–14v?
next_steps: Request a complete foliation-aware surrogate or consult the manuscript onsite.
Determine whether the difference reflects text extent, later matter, foliation, or catalog inference before normalizing a single range.

open_questions.question: How should Princeton's current catalog place/date statement, the manuscript's Merseyside ownership and production history, and the Taylor romance witnesses' LALME dialect evidence be reconciled?
next_steps: Consult recent codicological and dialect studies of Taylor MS. 9 before normalizing a narrower production place.
Keep witness production and dialect evidence separate from composition places for the three romances.

open_questions.question: Can complete, foliation-stable facsimiles of Lincoln Cathedral MS 91 and Bodleian MS. Douce 324 be obtained for direct comparison?
next_steps: Request complete present-object imaging of Lincoln MS 91 through the cathedral's researcher service and map Awntyrs ff. 154r–161r after delivery.
Request complete MS. Douce 324 imaging through Bodleian Imaging Services and confirm the exact Awntyrs locus.
Retain Lincoln's public f. 80r Morte Arthure image as zero-overlap partial coverage and Bodleian's fols. 1r/4v selection as partial noncontinuous coverage.
